cuisine: Commercial carbonated beverage — global brand sold in Singapore
region_of_origin: United States of America; created by The Coca-Cola Company in 1961 as a lemon-lime carbonated soft drink to compete with 7-Up. Widely distributed in Singapore and Southeast Asia.
food_subgroup: Carbonated sugar-sweetened lemon-lime soft drink; 1 standard can = 330 ml; Nutri-Grade D (Singapore HPB classification); ultra-processed.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a class for carbonated soft drinks generally (FOODON_03301638); no Sprite-specific entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category AN000A covers carbonated non-alcoholic soft drinks; no brand-specific Sprite code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 covers carbonated soft drinks; no Sprite-specific concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 standard aluminium can containing 330 ml (approximately 330 g) of Sprite.

nutrient_source.title: Sprite Lemon-Lime Carbonated Soft Drink — USDA Branded Food entry; supplemented by Coca-Cola Singapore product Nutrition Information Panel (330 ml can)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Branded Foods) — primary; Coca-Cola Singapore NIP — confirmatory
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA Branded Foods contains multiple Sprite entries (e.g. FDC ID 2346498, Sprite Lemon-Lime Soda) reporting energy 42 kcal/100 ml, carbohydrate 10 g/100 ml, sugar 10 g/100 ml, protein 0 g, fat 0 g, sodium ~3.9 mg/100 ml — consistent with the Coca-Cola Singapore 330 ml can NIP. Values confirmed as exact match. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation (carbonated water + sugar + flavourings only). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported on the product NIP or USDA entry for this product; -1 used per Veritas convention for unreported nutrients.

gi_source.title: Fructose, sucrose and glucose polymer drinks — GI values for sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages; Sprite lemon-lime soda GI ~63–90 range; representative value 90 used for sucrose-based carbonated lemon-lime drink in liquid form
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists GI values for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks in the range of 63–90 depending on sugar composition and carbonation. Sprite is a sucrose-based carbonated beverage; sucrose in liquid form with no fibre or fat buffer is rapidly absorbed. A GI of 90 is used as the representative high-end value consistent with published data for sucrose-sweetened carbonated drinks in the Sydney GI Database. GL per 330 ml serving = (90 × 33 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 29.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 3.9 mg/100 g; 13 mg per 330 ml can
hypertension.guidance: Sprite contains only 13 mg sodium per 330 ml can, which is negligible from a blood pressure management perspective; however, regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with weight gain, which is a modifiable risk factor for hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: 33 g free sugar per 330 ml can; GI ~90; GL ~30; Nutri-Grade D
type2_diabetes.guidance: One 330 ml can of Sprite delivers 33 g of free sugar (approximately 8.25 teaspoons) with a glycaemic index of ~90 and glycaemic load of ~30 — liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly with no fibre or fat buffer, producing a sharp postprandial glucose spike; people with or at risk of Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption and replace with plain water or unsweetened sparkling water. The common misconception that Sprite is 'lighter' or 'safer' than cola is clinically unfounded — Sprite (~33 g sugar/330 ml) and Coca-Cola (~35 g sugar/330 ml) have nearly identical sugar loads.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (33 g/can); zero saturated fat; Nutri-Grade D
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sprite contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, but its high free-sugar content (33 g per 330 ml can) contributes to excess caloric intake, hypertriglyceridaemia, and visceral adiposity — all established cardiovascular risk factors; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk independent of total caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sprite is a clear, lemon-lime flavoured carbonated soft drink manufactured by The Coca-Cola Company, first introduced in the United States in 1961 and now one of the most widely consumed carbonated beverages in Singapore and globally. It is sold in Singapore in 330 ml aluminium cans, 500 ml PET bottles, and larger formats, and carries a Nutri-Grade D classification under Singapore's HPB Nutri-Grade labelling scheme — the lowest grade, indicating high sugar content. Sprite is produced by dissolving sucrose in carbonated water with citric acid, sodium citrate, and natural lemon-lime flavourings; it contains no caffeine and no phosphoric acid, distinguishing it from cola drinks. The beverage is clear and colourless with a sharp, effervescent lemon-lime taste and a clean, slightly tart finish. Each 330 ml can contains approximately 33 g of free sugar (sucrose), equivalent to about 8.25 teaspoons, and delivers 139 kcal. Clinically, Sprite is one of the most deceptive beverages in Singapore's dietary landscape: its clear appearance and lemon-lime flavour profile lead many patients, parents, and caregivers to perceive it as 'lighter' or 'healthier' than cola drinks. This perception is a well-documented misconception — Sprite's sugar content per 330 ml (33 g) is nearly identical to Coca-Cola (approximately 35 g). The absence of caffeine and phosphoric acid are genuine differences but do not reduce the sugar load. A deeply embedded piece of Singapore household medical folklore is the use of Sprite to treat nausea — patients recovering from surgery, experiencing morning sickness, or suffering from gastric discomfort are commonly advised by family members to drink Sprite. Plain sparkling water or ginger tea provides equivalent symptomatic relief without the 33 g sugar burden. The glycaemic index of Sprite is approximately 90 (high), and the glycaemic load per 330 ml serving is approximately 30, making it a high-impact beverage for postprandial blood glucose. People with Type 2 Diabetes, prediabetes, or metabolic syndrome should avoid regular consumption.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values confirmed as exact match between USDA Branded Foods (Sprite Lemon-Lime Soda) and Coca-Cola Singapore 330 ml can NIP. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sucrose-based carbonated soft drinks. All mandatory fields complete.
notes: Carbonated lemon-lime sugar-sweetened soft drink; ultra-processed commercial product. Protein_g is confirmed zero — Sprite contains no protein by product formulation (carbonated water, sugar, citric acid, flavourings only). Fat_g is confirmed zero — Sprite contains no fat by product formulation. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are reported as -1 because neither the USDA Branded Foods entry nor the Coca-Cola Singapore NIP reports these sub-fractions for this product; they are structurally zero given fat_g = 0 but are left as -1 per Veritas convention for unreported fields. Water_g is reported as -1 because water content is not explicitly stated on the product NIP; the remainder after accounting for carbohydrate and minerals is approximately 89–90 g/100 g but this is not confirmed by the source. Nutri-Grade D per Singapore HPB classification. Common misconception: Sprite is not a healthier alternative to cola — sugar content is nearly identical.
